---
title: "Causal Impact of Digital Advertising on User Conversion"
author: "Jadon Freed"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: 
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    code_folding: hide
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(here)

here::i_am("notebooks/Causal_Analysis_Report.Rmd")
```

# Background
In 2024, global digital advertising spend reached over $740 billion. However, measuring the true Return on Investment (ROI) of ad campaigns is notoriously difficult due to selection bias, where advertisers often target users who were already likely to purchase.

This report analyzes the Criteo Uplift Dataset (comprising millions of user interactions) to move beyond naive correlation and answer a fundamental business question: Do digital advertisements causally increase the probability of a user visiting a website?

By utilizing Propensity Score Matching (PSM), Inverse Probability Weighting (IPW), and K-Means clustering, we isolated the causal Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) and identified high-value sub-populations to optimize future ad spend.

# Exploratory Data Analysis
```{r}
# Display the correlation plot generated by 02_eda.R
knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "correlation_plot.png"))
```

# Global Causal Impact (ATT)

To account for selection bias in who was targeted by the ad campaign, we implemented two robust causal inference techniques.Propensity Score Matching (PSM)We matched treated users to control users with similar baseline covariates to simulate a Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT). Covariate balance was successfully achieved:

```{r}
# Display the Love Plot generated by 03_psm_ipw.R
knitr::include_graphics(here("outputs", "figures", "psm_love_plot.png"))
```


Global Results
Both causal estimators confirmed a statistically significant, but globally small, positive causal effect of advertising on visit probability.
Naive Estimator: $0.92\%$
PSM ATT: $0.97\%$
IPW ATT: $0.71\%$


# Heterogeneous Treatment Effects (CATT)
Because the global causal impact was less than $1\%$, we hypothesized that budget was being wasted on unresponsive users. We applied K-Means clustering ($K=4$) to user baseline features to estimate the Conditional Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (CATT) for distinct user segments.


```{r}
# Load the results generated by 04_clustering.R
cluster_results <- read.csv(here("outputs", "cluster_att_results.csv"))

cluster_results %>%
  mutate(
    Percent_Users = scales::percent(Percent_Users, accuracy = 0.1),
    ATT_Estimate = scales::percent(ATT_Estimate, accuracy = 0.01),
    CI = paste0("[", round(CI_Lower, 4), ", ", round(CI_Upper, 4), "]")
  ) %>%
  select(Cluster, N_Users, Percent_Users, ATT_Estimate, CI) %>%
  kable(col.names = c("Cluster", "User Count", "% of Total Audience", "Causal Lift (CATT)", "95% Confidence Interval"),
        caption = "Causal Advertising Lift by User Segment") %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover", "condensed"), full_width = F) %>%
  row_spec(which.max(cluster_results$ATT_Estimate), bold = T, color = "white", background = "#2c3e50")
```

# Recommendations
## Target the High-Value Segment: 
The highlighted cluster represents a specific sub-population whose causal response to advertising is roughly five times greater than the global average.

# Reallocate Inefficient Spending: 
Over 50% of the audience showed a negligible causal response. Eliminating ad spend for these non-responders would drastically increase overall marketing ROI without sacrificing actual site visits.
